sourceEvidence: KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf, PDF page 42 (KNPC 2021 EXAM QUESTIONS, printed page 4), rendered at 200dpi. The paper prints verbatim: "Q13: When a software keeps changing during its development" / "A. Software Consistency" / "B. Software Stability" / "C. Software Durability" / "D. Software Testability". No blank, no underscores, no colon, no ellipsis, no trailing clause on the page image. It is a bare fragment in the source itself, exactly as we shipped it. Directly above it, Q12 is printed in the same fragment style: "Q12: what is changing one requirement, while taking into account competing requirements" with options A. Software Feasibility / B. Software Consistency / C. Software Maintainability / D. Software Stability. Note on the key (NOT changed in this pass): every keyed question on this page is marked with bold+underline on the correct option (e.g. Q10 "C. Tea, Orange, Tea, Tea, Apple" is bold+underlined). Q13's options carry NO bold and NO underline, so the source records no answer for Q13, which matches keyProvenance:'derived'.
note: Stem is verbatim from the source; the paper itself prints a bare fragment with no key marked, so nothing to repair here (key concern belongs to the key pass).

sourceEvidence: KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf, PDF page 39 (KNPC 2021 EXAM QUESTIONS, printed page 1), rendered at 200dpi. The paper prints verbatim: "Q4: max signed decimal number represented in 6 bits 1s complement?" / "A. -63 to +63" / "B. -31 to +32" / "C. -31 to +63" / "D. -31 to +31" (option D is bold+underlined, i.e. keyed in the source) / "Reference: https://byjus.com/question-answer/the-range-of-numbers-that-can-be-represented-by-6-bit-1-s-complement-number/". The word "range" is genuinely absent from the printed stem, and the printed options are genuinely ranges. The mismatch between the stem and the options exists in the paper, not in our extraction.
note: Source's own typo/sloppiness (stem says 'max ... number', options are ranges); kept verbatim per fidelity rule. Key D is bold+underlined in the source, so the shipped key is correct.

sourceEvidence: KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf, PDF page 46 (KNPC 2021 EXAM QUESTIONS, printed page 8), rendered at 200dpi. The paper prints verbatim: "Q27: Advantage of RISC processors" / "A. Simple instructions" (bold+underlined) / "B. Can operate on memory operands" / "C. Many instructions". There is no option D on the page - the list ends at C and is followed by "Reference:" plus a pasted 'RISC Advantages' slide image ("RISC have fewer and simpler instructions..."). No option was dropped during extraction; the source item only ever had three options.
note: Three options is what the paper prints; nothing was lost in extraction and the keyed option A is bold+underlined in the source.

sourceEvidence: KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf, PDF page 32 (KNPC 2016 EXAM, printed page 5), rendered at 200dpi. The paper prints verbatim: "29) why is IP used that ether net address?" / "a) Hieratical" (bold+underlined) / "b) Flat". Both the mangled stem and the misspelling "Hieratical" (for 'Hierarchical') are in the printed source, character for character, including the lowercase 'why' and the split 'ether net'. Our shipped stem and options reproduce the page exactly. Neighbouring items on the same page show the same crowd-recall sloppiness (e.g. "28) to know that the computer is connected to gate way?" / "a) ping gate way IP").
note: Garbling is in the paper itself, not our extraction; kept verbatim per the fidelity rule. Flagging for a human: the source stem is barely parseable and the keyed option text is a misspelling, so this item reads badly even though it is faithful.

sourceEvidence: KOC 2012 EXAM (2).pdf, PDF page 30 (KNPC 2016 EXAM, printed page 3), rendered at 200dpi. The paper prints verbatim: "10)  What is the network part that handles resource sharing?" / "a) IOS" / "b) Network operation system" (bold+underlined) / "c) Router" / "d) I don't remember". Option d really is printed as "I don't remember" (curly apostrophe) in the source document - it is not a note that leaked in during our extraction. The same placeholder appears elsewhere in the same paper, e.g. question 1 on PDF page 28: "1) A small company decided to go with windows 2000 servers ... d) I don't remember".
note: The 'I don't remember' option is printed in the paper itself (the recollector could not recall distractor d); kept verbatim, already carries the hedged_option flag.
